notable.notable_blurb: Dutch Governor of Demerara 1765-1770; NYC merchant

narrative_blocks.body: Jan Cornelis Van den Heuvel served as Governor of the Dutch province of Demerara (in present-day Guyana) from 1765 to 1770 — succeeding Laurens Lodewijk van Bercheijk and preceded Paulus van Schuylenburgh. Demerara was at the time one of the Dutch West India Company's principal sugar-producing colonial territories in South America. After his five-year governorship he relocated to New York City and continued as a merchant with the Dutch West India Company.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Margaret married Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll, 1814
narrative_blocks.body: Jan Cornelis's daughter Margaret Catherine Eleanor Van den Heuvel (1788-1878) married Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll in 1814 — bringing the Dutch colonial governor's bloodline into the Connecticut Ingersoll-Hooker tree. Through Margaret he became grandfather of seven, including Yale lawyer John Van den Heuvel Ingersoll (drowned Lake Erie 1846), US Rep Colin Macrae Ingersoll, and Governor of Connecticut Charles Roberts Ingersoll. The Van den Heuvel name persisted in Ingersoll descendants for multiple generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Hamilton, Astor, and Halleck grandchildren elsewhere
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond the Ingersoll-Hooker line, Jan Cornelis was grandfather (through daughter Maria) to fourteen Hamilton grandchildren — children of Maria's husband John Church Hamilton (son of Alexander Hamilton). These included Civil War General Alexander Hamilton (1815-1907), Schuyler Hamilton (1822-1903 of the Mexican War), and Elizabeth Hamilton (1831-1884) who married Henry Wager Halleck. Through daughter Susan he was also grandfather to Charlotte Augusta Gibbes who married John Jacob Astor III.

bio.display_name: Baron Jan Cornelis Van den Heuvel
bio.title: Baron

focus.n: Baron Jan Cornelis Van den Heuvel

parents.mother_research_notes: Daughter of NY landowner Charles Ward Apthorp

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: US Minister to Russia under Polk; Congressman 1825-33

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, 3 Maple Ave East

narrative_blocks.header: US Minister to Russia under President Polk
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll was appointed by Democratic President James K. Polk as the sixteenth United States Minister Plenipotentiary to the Russian Empire on August 8, 1846. He presented his credentials in Russia on May 30, 1847, and served until his resignation on July 1, 1848. The appointment was unsought and unexpected — he served his government 'faithfully and ably' before gladly returning to his New Haven law practice.

Ralph served four consecutive terms in the United States Congress representing Connecticut at-large from March 4, 1825 through March 3, 1833 — first elected as an Adams candidate, then reelected as Anti-Jacksonian. He served on the powerful Ways and Means committee for four years, holding the second-ranking position during his final term. He was not a candidate for renomination in 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined US Senate seat from Governor Edwards, 1835
narrative_blocks.body: In 1835 Governor Henry W. Edwards offered Ralph the United States Senate seat made vacant by the death of Senator Nathan Smith. He peremptorily declined — though the legislature would undoubtedly have elected him to the position. He similarly declined repeated gubernatorial nominations from his party across many years, departing from his settled purpose only once when Polk appointed him to Russia.

narrative_blocks.header: Best public speaker his contemporaries ever knew
narrative_blocks.body: One of Ralph's contemporaries with wide experience said of him 'he was the best public speaker I ever knew.' His voice was pleasant and almost musical, with unusual compass — distinct in its lowest tones. He was eminently persuasive before a jury or popular assembly, with a documented personal magnetism that allowed him to sway crowds, by turns humorous and pathetic, sarcastic when needed.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1808, New Haven law, Grove Street burial
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph graduated from Yale College in 1808 and read law for two years with Seth P. Staples before opening his New Haven practice. He married Margaret Catherine Eleanor Van den Heuvel in 1814 and they had seven children together — including future Governor of Connecticut Charles Roberts Ingersoll and US Congressman Colin Macrae Ingersoll. He died in New Haven August 26, 1872 at age eighty-three, buried at Grove Street Cemetery.

institutions.notes: Studied at Yale Law School after Yale College 1808

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; US Rep NY

narrative_blocks.body: Colin Macrae Ingersoll served two terms as a United States Congressman from Connecticut's 2nd district from March 4, 1851 through March 3, 1855 — succeeding Walter Booth, succeeded by John Woodruff. He continued the Ingersoll family's third generation in Congress alongside his father Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll (CT Congressman 1825-1833) and his cousin Charles Jared Ingersoll of Philadelphia. Connecticut had three concurrent Ingersolls in mid-nineteenth-century politics including his brother Charles R. Ingersoll as Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of legation St Petersburg during father's ministry
narrative_blocks.body: Colin served as Secretary of the United States legation at St. Petersburg Russia 1847-1848 — during his father Ralph Isaacs Ingersoll's term as US Minister to Russia under President Polk. In 1848 he served as acting Chargé d'Affaires at the St Petersburg post. The Ingersoll father-son diplomatic Russia posting was a rare American family pattern, taking both generations into the Tsarist court.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Julia Harriet Pratt, daughter of Zadock Pratt
narrative_blocks.body: Colin married Julia Harriet Pratt in 1853 — daughter of US Representative Zadock Pratt of New York, the tanner-banker who built the world's largest tannery at Prattsville NY in 1824. The marriage united two American congressional families. Their four children included future US Minister to Siam George Pratt Ingersoll (1861-1927).

narrative_blocks.header: Died age eighty-four pneumonia, same year as brother
narrative_blocks.body: Colin Macrae Ingersoll died September 13, 1903 in New Haven Connecticut at age eighty-four — the same year his brother Charles Roberts Ingersoll (Governor of Connecticut 1873-1877) also died in January 1903. The two Ingersoll brothers were both interred at Grove Street Cemetery New Haven. The cause of death was pneumonia.

institutions.notes: Yale College + Yale Law School graduate 1839
Trinity College 1839; founding member Phi Kappa Society

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

cross_connections.display_label: New Haven attorney; Bristol law firm founder, almost lifelong friend
cross_connections.notes: Lifelong friendship documented in Bristol's CT Reports memorial. Bristol senior counsel for Yale University, founder of Bristol Stoddard & Bristol 1888.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; US Navy Rear Admiral

narrative_blocks.body: Charles Roberts Ingersoll served as Governor of Connecticut from 1873 through 1877 — four years that placed him at the head of the state during Reconstruction-era industrial expansion. He continued the family's prominent role in Connecticut public life that his father Ralph (US Minister to Russia, four-term Congressman) and grandfather Jonathan Ingersoll Jr. (Lieutenant Governor 1816-1823) had established before him. Three consecutive generations of Ingersolls held principal Connecticut state offices.

narrative_blocks.header: Almost lifelong friendship with Louis H. Bristol
narrative_blocks.body: Charles maintained an unusually close, almost lifelong friendship with New Haven attorney Louis H. Bristol — the senior counsel for Yale University and founder of the Bristol Stoddard Bristol law firm. The friendship was described as 'one of special closeness' that ran from their early careers at the New Haven bar through to Bristol's 1910 death. Both men were New Haven figures of the same generation, both descendants of prominent Connecticut legal families.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Admiral Francis Gregory's daughter Virginia
narrative_blocks.body: Charles married Virginia Gregory (1823-1898), daughter of Admiral Francis Gregory of the United States Navy. The couple had five children together — only three of whom survived to adulthood, two infant deaths and a childhood death marking the family. Virginia died November 30, 1898 in New Haven at age seventy-five, five years before Charles followed in 1903.

Charles Roberts Ingersoll was born September 16, 1821 in North Haven Connecticut and died January 25, 1903 in North Haven at age eighty-one. He is buried at Grove Street Cemetery in New Haven alongside three generations of the Ingersoll family. The Grove Street cluster includes his father Ralph, grandfather Jonathan Jr., his wife Virginia Gregory, and his children.

institutions.notes: Yale College class of 1840; honorary LLD 1874
